perturbation.original_text: Distributor has the right to cancel, reschedule or modify all or any portion of a purchase order that has been accepted by Xplore at no cost to Distributor only if such cancellation or modification request is made within [*****] business days of PO acceptance or rescheduling request is made at least [*****] business days prior to the most current Estimated Shipping Date for that purchase order.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor has the right to cancel, reschedule or modify all or any portion of a purchase order that has been accepted by Xplore at no cost to Distributor only if such cancellation or modification request is made within one (1) business day of PO acceptance or rescheduling request is made at least one (1) business day prior to the most current Estimated Shipping Date for that purchase order.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cancellation window to 'one business day' after PO acceptance is commercially unreasonable and likely unenforceable, especially when dealing with manufacturing lead times and potential supply chain disruptions. This creates an unreasonable burden on the distributor and may conflict with general contract law principles of fairness and good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires that action be taken within a reasonable time, and the contract should give reasonable notification before cancellation. Reducing the distributor's time to cancel or modify an order to one business day is likely not considered reasonable and violates the spirit of good faith as required by the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the distributor's cancellation window to one business day, which is likely unreasonable under UCC standards, thus creating an inconsistency. The Law Explanation states that UCC § 2-309 requires action to be taken within a reasonable time and the contract should give reasonable notification before cancellation. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by noting that reducing the cancellation window is commercially unreasonable and creates an unreasonable burden, potentially conflicting with fairness and good faith principles. The scraped law snippet 2 discusses the UCC and its articles, reinforcing the relevance of UCC principles. The original text had a longer period ([*****] business days).

perturbation.original_text: If inventory levels fall below the thirty (30) day goal for more than sixty (60) consecutive days, Xplore, upon written notice to Distributor, shall replenish the stock to an amount agreed by both Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: If inventory levels fall below the thirty (30) day goal for more than one hundred eighty (180) consecutive days, Xplore, upon written notice to Distributor, may choose not to replenish the stock.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall replenish' to 'may choose not to replenish' and extending the period to 180 days allows Xplore to potentially breach its implied duty of good faith and fair dealing by failing to maintain adequate supply, potentially harming the distributor's ability to meet customer demands. This can be considered an unfair business practice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 states that every contract or duty within the UCC imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. By changing the term from 'shall replenish' to 'may choose not to replenish', Xplore is possibly violating their duty to act in good faith. This is because they can choose to not replenish the stock, thus harming the distributor's capacity to meet client requests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: and Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act in 1906; the Uniform Bills of Lading Act and Uniform Stock Transfer Act in 1909; and the Uniform Conditional Sales Act in 1918. The ULC officially took on the task of drafting a comprehensive code to provide guidelines for all commercial transactions in 1940. In 1942, the ULC and the American Law Institute joined in a partnership that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. perturbation.justification: The original contract requires Xplore to replenish stock if inventory levels fall below a certain threshold. The modified contract allows Xplore to choose not to replenish the stock, thus creating a legal contradiction under the UCC, as explained by the LLM. The LLM states that UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. By potentially failing to maintain adequate supply, Xplore risks violating its duty to act in good faith, which contradicts the UCC. The scraped snippet 2 supports this, highlighting the UCC's role in providing guidelines for all commercial transactions. The change falls under the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition, because it sets a requirement (replenishment of stock) that can be violated due to the modification.

perturbation.original_text: Shipping Terms. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in the Distribution Agreement, and unless notified by Xplore otherwise, shipping terms for Xplore Products will be Delivery Duty Paid (DDP) INCOTERMS® 2010, whereby Distributor's price, includes all costs of delivery, insurance, import and / or export duties and tariffs. Such prices are exclusive of all federal, state, municipal or other government excise, sales, use, occupational or like taxes in force, and any such taxes shall be assumed and paid for by Distributor in addition to its payment for the Xplore Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Shipping Terms. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in the Distribution Agreement, and unless notified by Xplore otherwise, shipping terms for Xplore Products will be Ex Works (EXW) INCOTERMS® 2010, whereby Distributor is responsible for all costs of delivery, insurance, import and / or export duties and tariffs, even if Xplore handles the shipping logistics. Such prices are exclusive of all federal, state, municipal or other government excise, sales, use, occupational or like taxes in force, and any such taxes shall be assumed and paid for by Distributor in addition to its payment for the Xplore Products. Xplore is not liable for any damage that may happen to the product during transit.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the INCOTERM from DDP (Delivery Duty Paid) to EXW (Ex Works) and adding that 'Xplore is not liable for any damage that may happen to the product during transit' places all responsibility for shipping and potential damage on the distributor. This is a substantial shift of risk and could be deemed unconscionable, especially if the distributor lacks the expertise to manage international shipping logistics or cannot secure adequate insurance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-303. Allocation or Division of Risks.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-303 discusses the allocation of risk. While parties can shift risk, this must be done in a way that is not unconscionable. Shifting all the burden of shipping and potential damage liability could be considered unconscionable if the distributor is inexperienced or unable to adequately manage the risks associated with international shipping, thus making the agreement unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The change from DDP to EXW INCOTERMS® 2010 significantly shifts the responsibility for shipping and potential damage from the seller (Xplore) to the distributor. The LLM-generated Law Explanation states that shifting all the burden of shipping and potential damage liability could be considered unconscionable if the distributor is inexperienced or unable to adequately manage the risks associated with international shipping, thus making the agreement unenforceable. The provided snippets discuss Article 2 of the UCC, governing the sale of goods and implicitly touch upon fairness in contractual terms. While there is no direct evidence of unconscionability, the shift is significant enough to classify as a legal contradiction because the distributor is now responsible for nearly everything, even if Xplore handles the shipping logistics.
